output:
## Solution

### Part 1: Number of cycles

Each cycle reduces capacity by 0.1%, so after *n* cycles:

$$\text{Capacity}(n) = 5000 \times 0.999^n$$

We need $5000 \times 0.999^n < 4500$, i.e., $0.999^n < 0.9$:

$$n > \frac{\ln(0.9)}{\ln(0.999)} = \frac{-0.10536}{-0.0010005} \approx 105.31$$

**After 106 complete cycles**, capacity drops below 4500 mAh.

Verification: 5000 × 0.999¹⁰⁵ ≈ 4501.5 mAh (still above) and 5000 × 0.999¹⁰⁶ ≈ 4497.0 mAh (below).

### Part 2: Total hours of use

Each cycle *i* (from 0 to 105) provides:

$$\text{Hours}_i = \frac{5000 \times 0.999^i}{500} = 10 \times 0.999^i$$

Total hours is the geometric series:

$$\text{Total} = 10 \sum_{i=0}^{105} 0.999^i = 10 \times \frac{1 - 0.999^{106}}{1 - 0.999} = \frac{10 \times (1 - 0.999^{106})}{0.001}$$

$$= 10{,}000 \times (1 - 0.89941) \approx \boxed{1005.9 \text{ hours}}$$

### Summary

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| Cycles to drop below 4500 mAh | **106** |
| Final capacity (at cycle 106) | ~4497 mAh |
| Total usable hours across all 106 cycles | **~1,005.9 hours** |

Note how the total is slightly less than the naive estimate of 106 × 10h = 1,060h — that ~54-hour gap is the cumulative effect of degradation.
